# Team Governance for PR Readiness (Optional)

## Purpose

Use this optional runbook when a team needs explicit PR readiness and approval rules in addition to the base workflow.

The goal is to reduce unclear ownership, inconsistent reviews, and unsafe merges in repositories where multiple contributors share implementation and release responsibility.

## When to use

Use this runbook when:

- more than one person contributes to the same repository;
- PR review quality is inconsistent;
- the team needs explicit Definition of Ready (DoR) and Definition of Done (DoD);
- merge responsibility is unclear;
- branch protection is limited by repository plan;
- escalation decisions are delayed or ad hoc.

If the project is maintained by one person, this runbook can be skipped.

## Governance scope

This runbook defines team policy for:

- PR readiness gates;
- reviewer ownership;
- approval and merge authority;
- escalation and exceptions;
- optional CODEOWNERS usage.

This runbook does not replace:

- `AGENTS.md` hard constraints;
- `runbooks/validation-checklist.md` evidence requirements;
- `runbooks/apply-starter-to-real-project.md` adoption sequence.

## Definition of Ready (DoR) for PR implementation

A PR is ready to implement only when all items below are explicit:

- requirement and expected behavior are clear;
- included scope and out-of-scope are documented;
- files likely to change are listed;
- validation plan is defined;
- known risks and assumptions are documented;
- selected PR is small enough to review safely.

Minimum evidence packet before implementation:

- requirement/spec/technical context reference;
- selected PR objective;
- acceptance criteria;
- validation expectations;
- branch policy (`no implementation on main`).

## Definition of Done (DoD) for merge readiness

A PR is done only when all mandatory checks pass:

1. Scope check
   - implementation stayed inside selected PR scope;
   - no hidden future-PR work included.
2. Review check
   - no unresolved High findings;
   - no unresolved Medium findings that affect correctness/safety.
3. Validation check
   - required commands/checks executed or explicitly justified as skipped;
   - evidence is explicit (commands, results, file references, manual checks).
4. Branch/merge check
   - implementation did not happen on `main`;
   - merge strategy is justified (`--squash` by default).
5. Documentation check
   - user-facing or workflow-impacting updates are documented when relevant.

## Reviewer ownership policy

### Roles

- **Author**: implements scoped change and prepares evidence.
- **Reviewer**: evaluates scope, risk, and validation evidence.
- **Approver/Merger**: merges only when DoD is met.

In small teams, reviewer and merger can be the same person, but the author must not self-approve unless an explicit exception is recorded.

### Reviewer checklist

- confirm selected PR scope was preserved;
- confirm changed files match expected scope;
- check severity-based findings;
- verify validation evidence quality;
- confirm merge recommendation is justified.

## Escalation policy

Escalate before merge when any of these conditions hold:

- unresolved High severity issue;
- unresolved Medium issue with correctness/security impact;
- missing validation evidence for risky change;
- required owner unavailable (domain/security/release);
- conflict between speed and safety decision.

Escalation path:

1. Author documents blocker and risk.
2. Reviewer classifies severity and impact.
3. Maintainer/tech lead decides one of:
   - block merge;
   - require scoped fix;
   - allow exception with explicit risk acceptance.

## Exception handling

When an exception is required, document in PR description:

- what rule is being bypassed;
- why it is necessary;
- risk introduced;
- mitigation plan;
- follow-up PR owner and deadline.

Do not merge with hidden exceptions.

## Optional CODEOWNERS adoption

Use CODEOWNERS when domain ownership should be enforced by default.

Suggested phased adoption:

1. Start with critical paths only (security, CI, deployment, auth).
2. Add feature/domain ownership after team agrees boundaries.
3. Keep ownership rules simple and review them periodically.

If CODEOWNERS is not available, apply equivalent reviewer ownership as team policy.

## Command examples

Author flow:

```bash
git switch -c <type>/<short-pr-slug>
npm run validate
git push -u origin <type>/<short-pr-slug>
gh pr create --base main --title "<title>" --body "<summary-and-evidence>"
```

Reviewer flow:

```bash
gh pr checkout <pr-number>
gh pr diff <pr-number>
gh pr review <pr-number> --approve
```

Merge flow:

```bash
gh pr merge <pr-number> --squash --delete-branch
git switch main
git pull
git fetch --prune
```

## Acceptance criteria for adopting this runbook

- DoR and DoD are visible to all contributors.
- Reviewer and merger responsibilities are explicit.
- Escalation path is known and used for blocked decisions.
- Exceptions are documented with risk and follow-up ownership.
- Merge policy is consistent with repository safety rules.
